title: Dora
content:
With farmer Allan at the farm abode
William and Dora. William was his son,
And she his niece. He often lookï¿½d at them,
And often thought, ï¿½Iï¿½ll make them man and wife.ï¿½
Now Dora felt her uncleï¿½s will in all,
And yearnï¿½d toward William; but the youth, because
He had been always with her in the house,
Thought not of Dora.
Then there came a day
When Allan callï¿½d his son, and said, ï¿½My son
I married late, but I would wish to see
My grandchild on my knees before I die
And I have set my heart upon a match.
Now therefore look to Dora; she is well
To look to; thrifty too beyond her age.
She is my brotherï¿½s daughter: he and I
Had once hard words, and parted, and he died
In foreign lands; but for his sake I bred
His daughter Dora: take her for your wife;
For I have wishï¿½d this marriage, night and day,
For many years.ï¿½ But William answerï¿½d short;
ï¿½I cannot marry Dora; by my life,
I will not marry Dora.ï¿½ Then the old man
Was wroth, and doubled up his hands, and said
ï¿½You will not, boy! you dare to answer thus!
But in my time a fatherï¿½s word was law,
And so it shall be now for me. Look to it;
Consider, William: take a month to think,
And let me have an answer to my wish;
Or, by the Lord that made me, you shall pack,
And never more darken my doors again.ï¿½
But William answerï¿½d madly; bit his lips,
And broke away. The more he lookï¿½d at her
The less he liked her; and his ways were harsh;
But Dora bore them meekly. Then before
The month was out he left his fatherï¿½s house,
And hired himself to work within the fields;
And half in love, half spite, he wooï¿½d and wed
A labourerï¿½s daughter, Mary Morrison.
Then, when the bells were ringing, Allan callï¿½d
His niece and said: ï¿½My girl, I love you well;
But if you speak with him that was my son,
Or change a word with her he calls his wife,
My home is none of yours. My will is law.ï¿½
And Dora promised, being meek. She thought,
ï¿½It cannot be: my uncleï¿½s mind will change!ï¿½
And days went on, and there was born a boy
To William; then distresses came on him;
And day by day he passï¿½d his fatherï¿½s gate,
Heart-broken, and his father helpï¿½d him not.
But Dora stored what little she could save.
And sent it them by stealth, nor did they know
Who sent it; till at last a fever seized
On William, and in harvest time he died.
Then Dora went to Mary. Mary sat
And lookï¿½d with tears upon her boy, and thought
Hard things of Dora. Dora came and said
ï¿½I have obeyï¿½d my uncle until now,
And I have sinnï¿½d, for it was all throï¿½ me
This evil came on William at the first.
But, Mary, for the sake of him thatï¿½s gone,
And for your sake, the woman that he chose,
And for this orphan, I am come to you
You know there has not been for these five years
So full a harvest: let me take the boy,
And I will set him in my uncleï¿½s eye
Among the wheat; that when his heart is glad
Of the full harvest, he may see the boy,
And bless him for the sake of him thatï¿½s gone.ï¿½
And Dora took the child, and went her way
Across the wheat, and sat upon a mound
That was unsown, where many poppies grew.
Far off the farmer came into the field
And spied her not; for none of all his men
Dare tell him Dora waited with the child;
And Dora would have risen and gone to him,
But her heart failï¿½d her; and the reapers reapï¿½d,
And the sun fell, and all the land was dark.
But when the morrow came, she rose and took
The child once more, and sat upon the mound;
And made a little wreath of all the flowers
That grew about, and tied it round his hat
To make him pleasing in her uncleï¿½s eye.
Then when the farmer passï¿½d into the field
He spied her, and he left his men at work,
And came and said: ï¿½Where were you yesterday?
Whose child is that? What are you doing here?ï¿½
So Dora cast her eyes upon the ground,
And answerï¿½d softly, ï¿½This is Williamï¿½s child!ï¿½
ï¿½And did I not,ï¿½ said Allan, ï¿½did I not
Forbid you, Dora?ï¿½ Dora said again
ï¿½Do with me as you will, but take the child,
And bless him for the sake of him thatï¿½s gone!ï¿½
And Allan said, ï¿½I see it is a trick
Got up betwixt you and the woman there.
I must be taught my duty, and by you!
You knew my word was law, and yet you dared
To slight it. Wellï¿½for I will take the boy;
But go you hence, and never see me more.ï¿½
So saying, he took the boy that cried aloud
And struggled hard. The wreath of flowers fell
At Doraï¿½s feet. She bowï¿½d upon her hands,
And the boyï¿½s cry came to her from the field,
More and more distant. She bowï¿½d down her head,
Remembering the clay when first shecame,
And all the things that had been. She howï¿½d down
And wept in secret; and the reapers reapï¿½d,
And the sun fell, and all the land was dark.
Then Dora went to Maryï¿½s house, and stood
Upon the threshold. Mary saw the boy
Was not with Dora. She broke out in praise
To God, that helpï¿½d her in her widowhood.
And Dora said, ï¿½My uncle took the boy
But, Mary, let me live and work with you
He says that he will never see me more.ï¿½
Then answerï¿½d Mary, ï¿½This shall never be,
That thou shouldst take my trouble on thyself
And, now I think, he shall not have the boy,
For he will teach him hardness, and to slight
His mother; therefore thou and I will go,
And I will have my boy, and bring him home;
And I will beg of him to take thee back
But if he will not take thee back again,
Then thou and I will live within one house,
And work for Williamï¿½s child, until he grows
Of age to help us.ï¿½
So the women kissï¿½d
Each other, and set out, and reachï¿½d the farm.
The door was off the latch: they peepï¿½d, and saw
The boy set up betwixt his grandsireï¿½s knees,
Who thrust him in the hollows of his arm,
And clapt him on the hands and on the cheeks,
Like one that loved him: and the lad stretchï¿½d out
And babbled for the golden seal, that hung
From Allanï¿½s watch, and sparkled by the fire.
Then they came in: but when the boy beheld
His mother, he cried out to come to her
And Allan set him down, and Mary said
ï¿½O Father!ï¿½if you let me call you soï¿½
I never carne a-begging for myself,
Or William, or this child; but now I come
For Dora: take her hack; she loves you well.
O Sir, when William died, he died at peace
With all men; for I askï¿½d him, and he said,
He could not ever rue his marrying meï¿½
I had been a patient wife: but, Sir, he said
That he was wrong to cross his father thus
ï¿½God bless him!ï¿½ he said, ï¿½and may he never know
The troubles I have gone throï¿½!ï¿½ Then he turnï¿½d
His face and passï¿½dï¿½unhappy that I am!
But now, Sir, let me have my boy, for you
Will make him hard, and he will learn to slight
His fatherï¿½s memory; and take Dora back,
And let all this be as it was before.ï¿½
So Mary said, and Dora hid her face
By Mary. There was silence in the room;
And all at once the old man burst in sobs:ï¿½
I have been to blameï¿½to blame. I have killï¿½d my son.
I have killï¿½d himï¿½but I loved himï¿½my dear son.
May God forgive me!ï¿½I have been to blame.
Kiss me, my children.ï¿½
Then they clung about
The old manï¿½s neck, and kissï¿½d him many times.
And all the man was broken with remorse;
And all his love came back a hundred-fold;
And for three hours he sobbï¿½d oï¿½er Wiliamï¿½s child
Thinking of William.
So those four abode
Within one house together; and as years
Went forward, Mary took another mate;
But Dora lived unmarried till her death.
meaning:
About: Alfred Tennyson, 1st Baron Tennyson (6 August 1809 ï¿½ 6 October 1892) was Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom and is one of the most popular English poets.

Much of his verse was based on classical mythological themes, although In Memoriam was written to commemorate his best friend Arthur Hallam, a fellow poet and classmate at Trinity College, Cambridge who was engaged to Tennyson's sister but died from a cerebral hemorrhage. One of Tennyson's most famous works is Idylls of the King (1885), a series of narrative poems based entirely on King Arthur and the Arthurian tales, as thematically suggested by Sir Thomas Malory's earlier tales on the legendary king. The work was dedicated to Prince Albert, the husband of Queen Victoria. During his career, Lord Tennyson attempted drama, but his plays enjoyed little success even in his lifetime.

Tennyson wrote a number of phrases that have become commonplaces of the English language, including: "nature, red in tooth and claw", "better to have loved and lost", "Theirs not to reason why, Theirs but to do and die", and "My strength is as the strength of ten, Because my heart is pure".

He is the second most frequently quoted writer in the English language, after Shakespeare.
